revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:円隆寺 (舞鶴市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Maizuru Enryu-ji 2019-05 ac (4).jpg|prefecture={{ill|Maizuru|qid=Q750573|1=Maizuru}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.44333267|N|135.32394409|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{Japan temple infobox | Name = Enryū-ji (円隆寺) | image = [[File:Maizuru Enryu-ji 2019-05 ac (4).jpg|280px]] | Location = {{ill|Maizuru|en|Maizuru|qid=Q750573|lt=Maizuru|1=Maizuru}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|35|26|36|N|135|19|26.2|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Mountain name = Jikei-san (慈恵山) | Sect = {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} | Principal image = {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}}, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, {{ill|Shaka Nyorai|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaka Nyorai|qq=}} | Founded = Traditionally during the Chōtoku period (995–999 CE) | Founder = Traditionally attributed to {{ill|Kōkei (monk)|en|Kōkei (monk)|qid=Q6455194|lt=Kōkei|1=Kōkei (monk)}} | Cultural Properties = Wooden seated statues of Amida Nyorai, Yakushi Nyorai, and Shaka Nyorai; standing statues of Fudō Myōō and Bishamonten (all designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) }} '''Enryū-ji''' (円隆寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Omuro School|ja|真言宗御室派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Omuro School|qq=}} located in {{ill|Maizuru|en|Maizuru|qid=Q750573|lt=Maizuru|1=Maizuru}}, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan. It is situated on the lower slopes of Mount Atago (愛宕山) overlooking the Nishimaizuru area. The mountain name (''sangō'') of the temple is Jikei-san (慈恵山). == History == According to the temple's own record, the ''Jikei-san-ki'' (慈恵山記), Enryū-ji was founded during the Chōtoku period (995–999 CE) by the monk {{ill|Kōkei (monk)|en|Kōkei (monk)|qid=Q6455194|lt=Kōkei|1=Kōkei (monk)}}.<ref name="JapanesePlaceNames">{{Cite book |title=日本歴史地名大系 京都府の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1981 |pages=668}}</ref> During its peak, the temple reportedly had 70 sub-temples. However, in 1595 (Bunji 4), a landslide destroyed its buildings. The temple was revitalized during the Edo period under the patronage of the {{ill|Makino clan|ja|牧野家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makino clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the lords of the {{ill|Tango-Tanabe Domain|en|Tango-Tanabe Domain|qid=Q10863423|lt=Tango Tanabe Domain|1=Tango-Tanabe Domain}}. Despite suffering fires in 1659 (Manji 2) and 1732 (Kyōhō 17), the current buildings were reconstructed in the following years.<ref name="JapanesePlaceNames" /> == Grounds == The current structures, including the main hall and the Tahōtō pagoda, date back to the late 18th century. They were built by local carpenters, including Dennojō Bōshō Hayashida. The architecture is noted for its originality, such as the tile-covered ''kamebara'' base of the Tahōtō. The ''sōmon'' (main gate) is unique with its triple roof design and enshrined statues of the {{ill|Four Heavenly Kings|en|Four Heavenly Kings|qid=Q1068367|lt=Four Heavenly Kings|1=Four Heavenly Kings}}. Visitors can also hike from the temple grounds to the summit of Mount Atago. Key structures include: * Main Hall (''Hondō'') * {{ill|Tahōtō|en|Tahōtō|qid=Q2451601|lt=Tahōtō|1=Tahōtō}} * Bell Tower (''Shōrō'') * ''Chinjudo'' shrine * Main Gate (''Sōmon'') * Abbot's Hall (''Hōjō'') - rebuilt in the mid-Edo period * Kitchen (''Kuri'') - rebuilt in the mid-Edo period * Goma Hall (''Gomadō'') - rebuilt in the mid-Edo period * Hall of Prince Shōtoku * Kannon Hall <gallery> Enryuji Maizuru04s3200.jpg|Main Hall Maizuru Enryu-ji 2019-05 ac (9).jpg|Tahōtō Pagoda Maizuru Enryu-ji 2019-05 ac (10).jpg|Bell Tower File:Enryuji Maizuru01s3200.jpg|Main Gate Maizuru Enryu-ji 2019-05 ac (7).jpg|Hall of Prince Shōtoku and Kannon Hall File:Asashiro-jinja02s3200.jpg|''Chinjudo'' </gallery> == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === * Wooden seated statues of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}}, {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, and {{ill|Shaka Nyorai|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaka Nyorai|qq=}} (Heian period). Designated on April 19, 1926.<ref name="kokuji">{{Cite web |url=https://www.ndl.go.jp/ |title=Official Gazette |date=April 19, 1926}}</ref> * Wooden standing statues of {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|qid=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} and {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. Designated on April 19, 1926.<ref name="kokuji" /> === Kyoto Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === Five structures built in the Edo period: * Main Hall (1786) * Tahōtō Pagoda (1751) * Bell Tower (1760) * ''Chinjudo'' shrine (1737) * Main Gate (1753) == Basic Information == ; Address * 72 Hikitsuchi, Maizuru City, Kyoto Prefecture, Japan ; Access * 10 minutes on foot from {{ill|Nishi-Maizuru Station|en|Nishi-Maizuru Station|qid=Q8192400|lt=Nishimaizuru Station|1=Nishi-Maizuru Station}} (JR Maizuru Line and Kyoto Tango Railway) * Parking available == Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Asashiro Shrine|ja|朝代神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asashiro Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Hongyō-ji|ja|本行寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongyō-ji|1=Hongyo-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11520793}} * {{ill|Keirin-ji (Maizuru)|ja|桂林寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keirin-ji (Maizuru)|1=Keirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11536791}} * {{ill|Zuikō-ji (Maizuru)|ja|瑞光寺 (舞鶴市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zuikō-ji (Maizuru)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == * ''Japanese Historical Place Names: Kyoto Prefecture,'' Heibonsha, 1981. ==References== {{reflist}}
